content.text: Message brokers decouple the services that produce events from those that consume them. RabbitMQ is a mature, open-source AMQP broker that implements this decoupling through a flexible routing layer — exchanges, queues, and bindings — that sits between producers and consumers. In agentic AI systems, where a single orchestrator might fan work out to tool workers, audit loggers, billing services, and vector-store writers simultaneously, RabbitMQ provides the reliable, ordered, at-least-once delivery guarantees those pipelines depend on.

RabbitMQ is a message broker built on the Advanced Message Queuing Protocol (AMQP 0-9-1). A broker acts as an intermediary: producers send messages to the broker, and the broker routes them to one or more queues where consumers fetch and process them. Unlike a direct point-to-point HTTP call, the producer never knows which consumer handles the message — the broker's routing rules decide.

The key entities in RabbitMQ are: producers (publishers), exchanges, bindings, queues, and consumers. A producer publishes a message to an exchange, supplying a routing key. The exchange inspects that key and its bindings to determine which queues receive a copy of the message. Consumers subscribe to queues and acknowledge (
) each message after successful processing. Unacknowledged messages are requeued or forwarded to a dead-letter queue (DLQ) depending on configuration.

RabbitMQ is written in Erlang, runs as a single node or a clustered deployment, and exposes its own management HTTP API plus a browser UI on port 15672. It supports multiple protocols via plugins: AMQP 0-9-1 (default), AMQP 1.0, MQTT, STOMP, and WebSockets. The ecosystem around it — client libraries, Kubernetes operators, cloud-managed offerings — is one of the most mature in the messaging space.

Synchronous request-response between microservices creates tight coupling: if the downstream service is slow or unavailable, the caller blocks or fails. For long-running agent operations — LLM inference, tool execution, vector search — blocking the orchestrator for every step wastes threads, inflates latency, and creates cascading failure under load.

RabbitMQ solves this by making communication asynchronous and durable. An orchestrator can publish a task message and immediately move on to the next request; a pool of agent workers drains the queue at whatever rate they can sustain. If all workers restart, messages wait in the queue — they are not lost. When a new feature needs to listen to existing events (e.g. adding a billing service that tracks
events), no producer code changes — the new service just binds a queue to the exchange with the right routing key.

In practice this matters for three concrete problems in agentic pipelines: fan-out (one event must reach multiple consumers), back-pressure (slow consumers must not overwhelm producers), and reliability (no message can be silently dropped even during worker restarts or broker failover).
— four exchange types (direct, topic, fanout, headers) cover virtually all routing patterns an agent pipeline needs without custom broker code.
— consumer acknowledgments ensure messages are not removed from the queue until a worker confirms successful processing. Crashed workers cause redelivery.
— messages that exhaust retries, exceed a TTL, or are explicitly nacked route to a DLQ for inspection, replay, or alerting rather than being silently discarded.
— queues can be declared with a
argument (1–255). Interactive agent sessions can be assigned higher priority than background batch jobs on the same queue.
— messages written to disk survive broker restarts; durable queues survive broker restarts. Both are one-line configuration.
— the built-in management UI shows per-queue depth, consumer counts, publish rates, and memory usage in real time. REST API enables automated alerting.
— logical partitions within a single RabbitMQ instance allow full isolation between production, staging, and development environments without separate clusters.
— clustering, quorum queue configuration, memory alarm thresholds, and disk alarms require more operational knowledge than a Redis-based queue.

— RabbitMQ is optimized for routing flexibility and low latency, not for the 10M+ messages-per-second immutable log append Kafka handles. For high-volume telemetry streams, Kafka or a cloud stream service is a better fit.
— unlike Kafka's append-only log, RabbitMQ queues discard messages once all bound consumers have acked them. Replay requires a DLQ or an external store.

— sending large LLM outputs or base64-encoded images as message bodies bloats queue memory and disk. The pattern is to store payloads in object storage (S3, GCS) and pass a reference ID in the message.

— the exchange/binding model is more powerful but also more nuanced than the simpler list-based queues in Redis Streams or SQS. Choosing the wrong exchange type or forgetting to bind a queue leads to silently dropped messages.
— migrating from classic mirrored queues to the recommended quorum queues (RabbitMQ 3.8+) requires queue-by-queue recreation; there is no in-place upgrade path.
When a producer publishes a message it opens a channel on an existing TCP connection and calls

with three key parameters: the exchange name, the routing key, and the message body (bytes). The broker receives the message, looks up the exchange, evaluates all bindings from that exchange to queues, and places copies in every matching queue. If no queue matches and the message is not marked mandatory, the broker silently drops it.
on a queue. The broker pushes messages to subscribed consumers up to the channel's prefetch window (
). A consumer processes the message and sends
to dead-letter). Unacked messages count against the prefetch window; once the window is full, the broker stops delivering new messages to that consumer — this is the back-pressure mechanism.
Each exchange type implements a different matching algorithm against the routing key:
— exact string match. Message routing key must equal the binding key exactly. Good for named task queues.
— ignores routing key entirely and delivers to every bound queue. Useful for broadcast notifications.
— matches on AMQP message headers instead of routing key. Rarely needed in agent pipelines; topic covers most cases.
Two orthogonal flags control durability. A queue declared with
survives a broker restart; its bindings are restored. A message published with

(PERSISTENT) is written to disk before the broker acknowledges receipt. Both must be set together for at-least-once delivery across restarts. A durable queue with non-persistent messages loses those messages on restart; a non-durable queue with persistent messages loses the queue and all its messages on restart.
A queue is configured as a DLQ source by setting two arguments at declaration time:
(the exchange to route dead letters to) and optionally
. A message is dead-lettered when: (1) a consumer nacks it with
; (2) its per-message or queue TTL expires; or (3) the queue reaches its
limit. The DLQ is itself a normal queue — consumers can monitor it for poison messages, replay them after fixes, or trigger alerts.

Classic mirrored queues replicate data to follower nodes via synchronous replication but have known failure modes (split-brain, unsynchronised mirrors). Quorum queues (introduced in RabbitMQ 3.8, recommended as default since 3.10) use the Raft consensus algorithm: a leader node receives writes and replicates to a quorum of followers before confirming. A quorum queue tolerates the loss of (n-1)/2 nodes without data loss. For production agent workloads, always prefer quorum queues.

The diagram below shows a typical RabbitMQ deployment for an agentic AI system. The orchestrator publishes to a topic exchange; bindings route messages to separate queues for agent workers, audit logging, and billing. Failed messages route to the dead-letter queue. The prefetch limit prevents any single slow consumer from monopolizing messages.

attrs.alt: RabbitMQ architecture diagram for agentic AI pipeline

content.text: Key points visible in the diagram: the exchange is the only component that knows routing rules — producers and consumers are unaware of each other. Multiple consumers can bind independent queues to the same exchange with different binding patterns, achieving fan-out without any producer code change. The DLQ sits outside the normal flow and is populated only on failure paths.
Your agent pipeline fans out a single event to multiple downstream services (audit, billing, analytics, vector indexing) with different routing rules.
Jobs must not be lost if a worker crashes — at-least-once delivery and durable queues are required.
You need a dead-letter queue to isolate and inspect poison messages without halting the main queue.
Different job types have different priorities — priority queues let interactive sessions jump ahead of batch jobs.
You need fine-grained access control between services using AMQP vhosts and user permissions.
Your message volume is in the tens of thousands per second range — RabbitMQ handles this comfortably.
You need event replay from the beginning of time — use Kafka or a cloud stream (Kinesis, Pub/Sub) instead; RabbitMQ deletes messages after consumption.
Throughput exceeds a few hundred thousand messages per second — Kafka's partitioned log model scales further horizontally.
The entire stack lives in one process or one small server and simplicity is paramount — a Python
or Redis list may be sufficient.
Messages are large binary blobs — store in S3 and pass a reference; RabbitMQ is not an object store.
The fastest path for local development is Docker. The official image includes the management plugin:

docker run -d --hostname rmq \
  -p 5672:5672 -p 15672:15672 \
  -e RABBITMQ_DEFAULT_USER=agent \
  -e RABBITMQ_DEFAULT_PASS=secret \
  rabbitmq:3.13-management

# Management UI: http://localhost:15672  (agent / secret)
# AMQP port: 5672
for async Python (FastAPI, asyncio workers) or

pip install aio_pika  # async
pip install pika       # sync (simpler for scripts)

Always declare exchanges, queues, and bindings idempotently at startup. Declaring an entity that already exists with the same parameters is a no-op; a mismatch raises a channel error. This means any service can declare its own resources without coordinating with others.

import asyncio
import aio_pika

async def setup_infrastructure(url: str) -> None:
    connection = await aio_pika.connect_robust(url)
    async with connection:
        channel = await connection.channel()

        # Topic exchange for all agent events
        exchange = await channel.declare_exchange(
            "agent.events",
            aio_pika.ExchangeType.TOPIC,
            durable=True,
        )

        # Dead-letter exchange
        dlx = await channel.declare_exchange(
            "agent.dlx", aio_pika.ExchangeType.DIRECT, durable=True
        )

        # Dead-letter queue
        dlq = await channel.declare_queue("agent.dlq", durable=True)
        await dlq.bind(dlx, routing_key="dlq")

        # Main agent-runs queue with DLQ routing on nack
        runs_queue = await channel.declare_queue(
            "agent.runs",
            durable=True,
            arguments={
                "x-dead-letter-exchange": "agent.dlx",
                "x-dead-letter-routing-key": "dlq",
                "x-message-ttl": 3_600_000,  # 1 hour in ms
                "x-queue-type": "quorum",    # Raft-based replication
            },
        )
        await runs_queue.bind(exchange, routing_key="run.*")

        # Audit queue receives all events
        audit_queue = await channel.declare_queue(
            "agent.audit", durable=True,
            arguments={"x-queue-type": "quorum"}
        )
        await audit_queue.bind(exchange, routing_key="#")

        print("Infrastructure declared.")

asyncio.run(setup_infrastructure("amqp://agent:secret@localhost/"))
(publisher confirms) for critical events so they know the broker persisted the message before moving on:

import aio_pika
import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone

async def publish_event(
    channel: aio_pika.Channel,
    exchange: aio_pika.Exchange,
    routing_key: str,
    payload: dict,
) -> None:
    body = json.dumps({
        **payload,
        "published_at": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
    }).encode()
    message = aio_pika.Message(
        body=body,
        delivery_mode=aio_pika.DeliveryMode.PERSISTENT,
        content_type="application/json",
        message_id=payload["run_id"],  # idempotency key
    )
    await exchange.publish(message, routing_key=routing_key)

# Usage
# await publish_event(channel, exchange, "run.started", {"run_id": "abc", "model": "claude-sonnet-4-5"})
Step 5: Consume with prefetch and acknowledgment

so each worker holds at most one unacked message at a time. This enforces fair dispatch: a slow worker stops receiving new messages until it acks the current one. Increase to 5–20 for lightweight tasks where the per-message overhead of a round-trip matters.

import aio_pika
import asyncio
import json
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

async def run_agent_worker(url: str) -> None:
    connection = await aio_pika.connect_robust(url, heartbeat=60)
    async with connection:
        channel = await connection.channel()
        await channel.set_qos(prefetch_count=1)

        queue = await channel.declare_queue("agent.runs", durable=True, passive=True)

        async with queue.iterator() as q:
            async for message in q:
                async with message.process(requeue=False):  # nack -> DLQ
                    try:
                        payload = json.loads(message.body)
                        run_id = payload["run_id"]
                        logger.info("Processing run %s", run_id)
                        await execute_agent(payload)
                        # context manager auto-acks on clean exit
                    except Exception:
                        logger.exception("Run failed; routing to DLQ")
                        raise  # re-raise causes nack -> DLQ

asyncio.run(run_agent_worker("amqp://agent:secret@localhost/"))
Example 1 — simple publish and consume (pika, synchronous)
This introductory example uses the synchronous
library — no asyncio needed — to verify basic connectivity and message round-trip:

import pika
import json

CONNECTION_PARAMS = pika.ConnectionParameters(
    host="localhost", port=5672,
    credentials=pika.PlainCredentials("agent", "secret")
)

def publish_simple(routing_key: str, payload: dict) -> None:
    conn = pika.BlockingConnection(CONNECTION_PARAMS)
    ch = conn.channel()
    ch.exchange_declare(exchange="agent.events", exchange_type="topic", durable=True)
    ch.queue_declare(queue="agent.runs", durable=True)
    ch.queue_bind(exchange="agent.events", queue="agent.runs", routing_key="run.*")

    ch.basic_publish(
        exchange="agent.events",
        routing_key=routing_key,
        body=json.dumps(payload).encode(),
        properties=pika.BasicProperties(delivery_mode=pika.DeliveryMode.Persistent),
    )
    print(f"Published: {routing_key} -> {payload}")
    conn.close()

def consume_one() -> dict:
    conn = pika.BlockingConnection(CONNECTION_PARAMS)
    ch = conn.channel()
    ch.basic_qos(prefetch_count=1)
    method, props, body = ch.basic_get(queue="agent.runs", auto_ack=False)
    if method:
        payload = json.loads(body)
        ch.basic_ack(method.delivery_tag)
        print(f"Consumed: {payload}")
        conn.close()
        return payload
    conn.close()
    return {}

# Run: publish then consume
publish_simple("run.started", {"run_id": "r001", "prompt": "Summarise this document"})
consume_one()
Example 2 — production priority queue with retry cap (aio_pika)
This realistic example sets up a priority queue for agent runs, with a retry count tracked in message headers. After three attempts the message is sent to the DLQ instead of being requeued:

import aio_pika
import asyncio
import json
import logging
from typing import Optional

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
MAX_RETRIES = 3
AMQP_URL = "amqp://agent:secret@localhost/"

async def setup_priority_queue(channel: aio_pika.Channel) -> aio_pika.Queue:
    exchange = await channel.declare_exchange(
        "agent.priority", aio_pika.ExchangeType.DIRECT, durable=True
    )
    dlx = await channel.declare_exchange(
        "agent.dlx", aio_pika.ExchangeType.DIRECT, durable=True
    )
    dlq = await channel.declare_queue("agent.dlq", durable=True)
    await dlq.bind(dlx, routing_key="dlq")

    queue = await channel.declare_queue(
        "agent.priority.runs",
        durable=True,
        arguments={
            "x-max-priority": 10,
            "x-dead-letter-exchange": "agent.dlx",
            "x-dead-letter-routing-key": "dlq",
            "x-queue-type": "classic",  # priority requires classic queue
        },
    )
    await queue.bind(exchange, routing_key="agent.priority.runs")
    return queue

async def publish_with_priority(
    channel: aio_pika.Channel, payload: dict, priority: int = 5
) -> None:
    exchange = await channel.declare_exchange(
        "agent.priority", aio_pika.ExchangeType.DIRECT, durable=True
    )
    msg = aio_pika.Message(
        body=json.dumps(payload).encode(),
        delivery_mode=aio_pika.DeliveryMode.PERSISTENT,
        priority=priority,
        headers={"retry_count": 0},
    )
    await exchange.publish(msg, routing_key="agent.priority.runs")

async def process_with_retry(message: aio_pika.IncomingMessage, channel: aio_pika.Channel) -> None:
    retry_count: int = message.headers.get("retry_count", 0)
    payload = json.loads(message.body)
    run_id = payload.get("run_id", "unknown")

    try:
        await execute_agent(payload)  # your actual agent call
        await message.ack()
        logger.info("Run %s completed", run_id)
    except Exception as exc:
        logger.warning("Run %s attempt %d failed: %s", run_id, retry_count + 1, exc)
        if retry_count >= MAX_RETRIES - 1:
            await message.nack(requeue=False)  # -> DLQ
        else:
            # Re-publish with incremented retry count and delay
            exchange = await channel.declare_exchange(
                "agent.priority", aio_pika.ExchangeType.DIRECT, durable=True
            )
            retry_msg = aio_pika.Message(
                body=message.body,
                delivery_mode=aio_pika.DeliveryMode.PERSISTENT,
                priority=message.priority or 5,
                headers={"retry_count": retry_count + 1},
                expiration=str(2 ** retry_count * 5000),  # exp backoff ms
            )
            await exchange.publish(retry_msg, routing_key="agent.priority.runs")
            await message.ack()  # remove original

async def execute_agent(payload: dict) -> None:
    """Placeholder — replace with real agent invocation."""
    await asyncio.sleep(0.1)

Consider a production document-processing pipeline where users upload PDFs, the system extracts text, runs an LLM to summarise, stores vectors, and bills the account. Each stage is an independent microservice that should scale independently and fail without blocking the others.
The orchestrator publishes to a topic exchange
). Each queue has a corresponding microservice consuming it.

# Orchestrator: document ingestion service
async def on_upload(doc_id: str, s3_key: str, channel, exchange) -> None:
    """Called when a user uploads a document. Publishes to pipeline."""
    await exchange.publish(
        aio_pika.Message(
            body=json.dumps({"doc_id": doc_id, "s3_key": s3_key}).encode(),
            delivery_mode=aio_pika.DeliveryMode.PERSISTENT,
        ),
        routing_key="doc.uploaded",
    )

# Extraction worker: consumes doc.* events
async def extraction_worker(message: aio_pika.IncomingMessage) -> None:
    async with message.process(requeue=False):
        data = json.loads(message.body)
        text = await extract_text_from_s3(data["s3_key"])
        await store_text(data["doc_id"], text)
        # Publish next stage
        await exchange.publish(
            aio_pika.Message(
                body=json.dumps({"doc_id": data["doc_id"]}).encode(),
                delivery_mode=aio_pika.DeliveryMode.PERSISTENT,
            ),
            routing_key="doc.extracted",
        )
At 50,000 documents per day the pipeline processes roughly 0.6 messages per second — well within RabbitMQ's single-node capacity. Each extraction worker runs with
since extraction is I/O-bound (S3 reads). The LLM summarisation worker uses

because LLM calls can take 20–60 seconds and should not time out while waiting for a second message. The billing service uses a separate vhost with its own credentials so a billing bug cannot corrupt pipeline queues.
When the LLM API is rate-limited, the summarisation worker raises an exception, nacks the message with
, and re-publishes with an exponential backoff header. After three retries the message reaches
, where an alert fires and an operator can inspect the failure and replay the message once the API recovers.

Auto-ack removes the message from the queue the moment it is delivered to the consumer, before the consumer has processed it. If the worker crashes during processing, the message is permanently lost. Always use manual acknowledgment:

# WRONG — auto_ack=True loses messages on crash
channel.basic_consume(queue="agent.runs", on_message_callback=callback, auto_ack=True)

# CORRECT — ack only after successful processing
def callback(ch, method, props, body):
    process(body)            # do work first
    ch.basic_ack(method.delivery_tag)   # then ack

Declaring a queue and declaring an exchange are independent steps. If the binding is missing, published messages match no queue and are silently dropped (or returned to the publisher if the message is published as mandatory). Always declare the binding explicitly in startup code and verify it in the management UI.
Sending a 2 MB LLM response as a message body bloats RabbitMQ's memory footprint, slows delivery, and risks hitting the broker's
limit. The correct pattern is to store the output in object storage and send only an identifier:

# WRONG
await exchange.publish(Message(body=llm_response_bytes), routing_key="run.completed")

# CORRECT
s3_key = await upload_to_s3(llm_response_bytes)
await exchange.publish(
    Message(body=json.dumps({"run_id": run_id, "result_s3_key": s3_key}).encode()),
    routing_key="run.completed",
)
Without a prefetch limit, RabbitMQ delivers all ready messages to the first consumer that connects — even if that consumer is already saturated. The other consumers in the pool starve. Always call

RabbitMQ guarantees at-least-once delivery: if the broker or consumer restarts between delivery and ack, the message is redelivered. Consumers that are not idempotent (e.g. they charge a user's account on every delivery) will process the same message multiple times. Use a deduplication key (
, stored in a Redis set or DB unique constraint) to skip already-processed messages.
Declare all infrastructure (exchanges, queues, bindings) idempotently at service startup, not lazily during message processing. This avoids race conditions and missing resources.
— so bindings can be as specific or broad as needed without changing producers.
on messages that must survive a broker restart, and declare queues as
. Setting one without the other gives false durability.
) in production clusters. Classic mirrored queues are deprecated and have known failure modes.
Keep messages small: include only identifiers and metadata. Fetch large payloads from object storage in the consumer. Target less than 64 KB per message.
Implement idempotent consumers using the message's
) stored in a fast lookup (Redis, Postgres unique index).
Monitor three metrics continuously: queue depth (
), and DLQ arrival rate. A rising queue depth with full consumer utilization means you need more workers.
Separate vhosts per environment (production, staging, development). This provides isolation at the credential and permission level without running separate clusters.
with a retry wrapper in pika) to automatically reconnect on network failures. Do not assume a connection is permanent.

A single RabbitMQ node can sustain roughly 20,000–50,000 small messages per second for durable queues (numbers vary with message size, persistence, and hardware). Non-persistent messages to transient queues can reach 100,000+ messages per second, but that level of throughput is rarely needed in agent pipelines.

The simplest scaling mechanism is adding consumer processes. Because queues implement a competing-consumer (round-robin) model, starting 10 consumer processes on a queue without changing any configuration gives roughly 10x throughput. Kubernetes horizontal pod autoscaling driven by RabbitMQ queue depth (via the
plugin and KEDA or a custom HPA metric) is the standard production approach.
The optimal prefetch count depends on the work profile. For CPU/IO-bound tasks where each message takes 100ms–10s,
maximizes fairness at modest throughput. For lightweight tasks where the AMQP round-trip overhead dominates,
reduces idle time. A rough rule: if consumer CPU utilization is below 50% with
and latency allows, increase to 5 and measure.
RabbitMQ has built-in flow control: when a node's memory usage exceeds the
(default 40% of RAM) or disk space falls below the

, it blocks publishing connections. This prevents the broker from running out of resources at the cost of increased producer latency. Monitor these thresholds with the Prometheus exporter and alert before they are hit.

A RabbitMQ cluster shares metadata (exchanges, queues, bindings, users) across nodes, but message data for classic queues lives on the node where the queue was declared. Quorum queues replicate message data across a configured set of nodes (typically 3). For high availability, run a 3-node cluster with quorum queues. Use a load balancer (HAProxy, AWS NLB) in front of the cluster for connection distribution.

Federation and shovel plugins allow queues to be linked across geographically separated clusters, forwarding messages from a remote queue to a local one. This is useful for multi-region agent deployments where each region runs its own RabbitMQ cluster but events must propagate globally.
— asyncio-native AMQP client, built on top of
. Recommended for FastAPI and asyncio agent services.
— synchronous AMQP client maintained by the RabbitMQ team. Best for scripts, Celery backends, and simple workers.
— task queue framework that can use RabbitMQ as its broker. Adds task tracking, result backends, and scheduling on top of raw AMQP.

# Enable Prometheus plugin (persists across restarts)
rabbitmq-plugins enable rabbitmq_prometheus

# Metrics available at http://localhost:15692/metrics
# Key metrics:
#   rabbitmq_queue_messages_ready       — depth ready to deliver
#   rabbitmq_queue_messages_unacked     — in-flight, unacknowledged
#   rabbitmq_queue_consumer_utilisation — 0.0-1.0 fraction of time consumers active
#   rabbitmq_queue_messages_published   — publish rate
#   rabbitmq_node_mem_used              — memory usage
KEDA (Kubernetes Event-Driven Autoscaler) can scale consumer deployments based on RabbitMQ queue depth. A
resource watches the queue via the RabbitMQ management HTTP API and adjusts replica count automatically:

apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
metadata:
  name: agent-worker-scaler
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: agent-worker-deployment
  minReplicaCount: 1
  maxReplicaCount: 20
  triggers:
  - type: rabbitmq
    metadata:
      host: amqp://agent:secret@rabbitmq:5672/
      queueName: agent.runs
      mode: QueueLength
      value: "10"   # scale up when depth > 10 per replica
— RabbitMQ as a service, free tier available, multi-cloud.
— managed RabbitMQ and ActiveMQ on AWS, VPC-native.
— not RabbitMQ but AMQP-compatible; drop-in for some workloads.
RabbitMQ Kubernetes Operator — official operator for deploying and managing RabbitMQ clusters on Kubernetes (

RabbitMQ supports username/password, x.509 certificates, LDAP, and OAuth 2.0 (via the rabbitmq-auth-backend-oauth2 plugin). In production, each microservice should have its own RabbitMQ user with minimal permissions: configure rights on its own queues, write rights on its exchange, and no access to other vhosts. The least-privilege principle limits blast radius when credentials are compromised.

# Create a dedicated user and limit permissions via CLI
rabbitmqctl add_user agent_worker s3cr3t
rabbitmqctl set_permissions -p /production agent_worker \
  '^agent\..*$'   \  # configure: can only declare/delete agent.* resources
  '^agent\..*$'   \  # write: can only publish to agent.* exchanges
  '^agent\..*$'      # read: can only consume from agent.* queues

Enable TLS on the AMQP listener (port 5671) for all production traffic. Use a CA-signed certificate or Let's Encrypt via a sidecar; avoid self-signed certs unless you pin the CA in the client. In aio_pika, pass
is fire-and-forget: the broker may lose the message if it runs out of disk space between receipt and persistence. Enable publisher confirms on the channel (
in pika) and await the broker's
before considering the message safe. This is essential for billing and audit events.
Classic mirrored queues are deprecated in RabbitMQ 3.9+ and removed in 4.0. Quorum queues use Raft consensus and are the correct HA primitive. They tolerate the loss of a minority of replicas (
nodes for a queue with n replicas). Declare them with
Queue depth growing unboundedly — consumers are too slow or have stopped. Alert when depth exceeds a threshold for more than 5 minutes.
DLQ accumulating messages — consumers are failing repeatedly. Alert and page on-call when DLQ receives messages.
Memory alarm triggered — broker is blocking publishers. Scale consumers or increase the broker's memory limit.
Consumer utilization at 1.0 — consumers are saturated; add replicas or increase prefetch (if task allows).

Think of RabbitMQ as a post office with an automated sorting facility. Producers drop parcels (messages) into the sorting machine (exchange) with an address label (routing key). The sorting machine reads the label and places copies of the parcel into the correct mailboxes (queues) based on address rules (bindings). Postal workers (consumers) empty their mailbox one parcel at a time, sign for each one (ack), and if a parcel cannot be delivered (nack), it goes to the undeliverable parcel room (DLQ) rather than being lost.

The key insight is that the sorting machine — the exchange — is where routing intelligence lives. Producers and consumers are oblivious to each other. Adding a new consumer is as simple as renting a new mailbox and telling the sorting machine which labels to copy there.
RabbitMQ is an AMQP 0-9-1 message broker whose exchange–binding–queue model provides flexible, configurable routing between producers and consumers without coupling them directly.
Topic exchanges with hierarchical routing keys (
) cover the fan-out and selective routing needs of most agentic AI pipelines.
At-least-once delivery requires three things together: durable queues, persistent messages, and manual consumer acknowledgment — missing any one of them creates a reliability gap.
Dead-letter queues are the correct way to handle poison messages — configure
on the source queue and monitor the DLQ for alerts.
Quorum queues (Raft-based, default since RabbitMQ 3.10) are the correct HA primitive in production clusters; classic mirrored queues are deprecated.
) is the primary back-pressure mechanism — set it to 1 for slow, long-running agent tasks and increase it for lightweight fast tasks.
Keep message bodies small (under 64 KB); store large LLM outputs in object storage and pass only a reference identifier in the message.
Use separate vhosts and dedicated users per service with least-privilege permissions; enable TLS on port 5671 and publisher confirms for critical events.

RabbitMQ excels at routed, reliable, low-to-medium volume job queues; prefer Kafka for high-volume immutable event logs that need replay, and Redis Streams for ultra-simple queue needs within an existing Redis deployment.
